stories.headline: Philippines marks 10 years of South China Sea ruling as 14 nations back award
stories.summary: The Philippines on July 12 marked the 10th anniversary of the 2016 arbitral award that rejected China's expansive South China Sea claims, with a National Peace Walk in Manila, a parade in Puerto Princesa and a nationwide sounding of navy ships' horns. In a joint statement, 14 governments — among them the Philippines, United States, Japan, Australia and the United Kingdom — called the ruling final and legally binding on Manila and Beijing and said China's claims have no legal basis under the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea.
stories.context: The Permanent Court of Arbitration in The Hague ruled on July 12, 2016 that China's nine-dash-line claim had no legal foundation. China has rejected the ruling as null and void and continues to assert control over waters within the Philippines' exclusive economic zone.

stories.headline: Impeachment court moves to speed up Duterte trial as week's witnesses are set
stories.summary: The Senate impeachment court is seeking to accelerate the trial of Vice President Sara Duterte, with Senator Panfilo Lacson saying witnesses could be kept in a Senate holding area during hearing days to avoid delays and warning that the current pace of one witness a week could stretch proceedings into December. Presiding officer Francis Escudero was open to speeding up the trial. The prosecution rescheduled NBI regional director Jeremy Lontoc to testify July 13, followed by Duterte's chief of staff Zuleika Lopez later in the week.
stories.context: The trial, which opened July 6, is the first of a sitting Philippine vice president and centers partly on alleged threats against President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. and other officials. A conviction requires a two-thirds Senate vote and would remove Duterte from office. She denies the accusations.
stories.watch_for: Hearings resume Monday, July 13, at 2 p.m., with the prosecution's witnesses scheduled through the week.

stories.headline: Inday and monsoon death toll rises to 18 as habagat rains begin to ease
stories.summary: The National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council said the combined effects of Typhoon Inday and the enhanced southwest monsoon had left 18 people dead, seven injured and 12 missing as of Sunday, July 12, with most deaths from drowning and landslides in Sarangani, Lanao del Sur and Bukidnon. It said about 123,000 families, or more than 562,000 people, had been affected. PAGASA said the monsoon was weakening after Typhoon Inday left the Philippine Area of Responsibility on July 11.
stories.watch_for: PAGASA said heavy rains could still affect the Ilocos Region on July 13 before conditions improve, and kept yellow rainfall warnings over parts of Luzon and the Visayas.

stories.headline: Marcos inspects P1.6-billion upgrade of Navotas Fish Port Complex
stories.summary: President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. inspected the ongoing rehabilitation of the Navotas Fish Port Complex on Saturday, July 11, calling the facility one of the country's main fish landing and trading centers. The Philippine Fisheries Development Authority and Department of Agriculture announced last month a P1.6-billion project to expand the complex by nearly 25 hectares and turn it into a wider trading hub for fish and farm goods. Marcos said a more modern port would make operations safer and more efficient and help ensure a steady food supply.

stories.headline: Military, security officials reject claim that Batanes belongs to China
stories.summary: The Armed Forces of the Philippines and the National Security Council rejected assertions by some Chinese academics that Batanes province has no legal basis to be under Philippine control and should belong to China. AFP spokesperson Rear Admiral Roy Vincent Trinidad called Batanes an integral part of the republic protected by international law and described the claims as foreign malign influence. Defense Secretary Gilberto Teodoro Jr. called the assertions baseless, and National Security Adviser Eduardo Oban warned against letting manufactured history justify coercion.
stories.context: The remarks came as the Philippines prepared to mark the 10th anniversary of the 2016 arbitral ruling against China's South China Sea claims. Batanes, the country's northernmost province, lies near the Luzon Strait between the Philippines and Taiwan.

stories.headline: Customs and NBI seize P980 million in smuggled cigarettes, vow wider crackdown
stories.summary: The Bureau of Customs and the National Bureau of Investigation said on Saturday, July 11, that they had inspected 25 newly seized container vans holding about P980 million worth of illicit cigarettes in Cebu, part of 59 containers intercepted in continuing anti-smuggling operations valued at an estimated P3 billion. Customs Commissioner Ariel Nepomuceno said the bureau would keep seizing illegal shipments and pursue those behind them, and NBI Director Melvin Matibag pledged closer coordination. The two agencies signed an agreement to strengthen intelligence-sharing.

stories.headline: US strikes Iran and Tehran fires on Gulf states as Hormuz crisis deepens
stories.summary: The United States said its military struck about 140 Iranian targets, including missile and drone sites and naval assets, after accusing Iran of attacking a Cyprus-flagged container ship in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's Revolutionary Guard said it had closed the strait to all traffic. Missiles and drones were fired at the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Kuwait, Oman and Bahrain overnight into Sunday, July 12; Qatar reported three people hurt by falling shrapnel and condemned what it called a dangerous escalation. Omani and Iranian officials said talks on the strait would continue.
stories.context: The Strait of Hormuz is a chokepoint for a large share of the world's seaborne oil trade. A ceasefire reached in June had briefly eased tensions before fighting resumed in early July.

stories.headline: Russian missile and drone barrage kills at least eight across Ukraine
stories.summary: Ukrainian officials said Russian missiles and drones killed at least eight people and wounded dozens across several cities on Saturday, July 11. Five were killed and about 30 wounded in Sumy, where glide bombs hit a crowded area, while strikes also hit Odesa, Kyiv, Kharkiv and Sloviansk. Russia used ballistic and cruise missiles and about 121 drones, President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said, adding that air defenses downed most projectiles but not the ballistic missiles. Ukraine said its own drones struck fuel tankers and support vessels in the Sea of Azov.

stories.headline: Ebola deaths in DR Congo reach 600 as outbreak spreads in the east
stories.summary: The Democratic Republic of the Congo's health ministry said confirmed Ebola deaths had reached 600, with about 1,759 confirmed cases, in an update on July 9 that recorded 51 new cases and 20 deaths in the previous day. The outbreak, declared on May 15 and caused by the Bundibugyo strain, is centered in three eastern provinces, with Ituri the hardest hit. There is no approved vaccine, though treatment trials were beginning enrollment. Health workers in Ituri have threatened to strike over unpaid wages.
stories.context: The World Health Organization declared the outbreak a public health emergency of international concern in May. The Bundibugyo strain is generally considered less deadly than some other Ebola variants.

stories.headline: Argentina beats Switzerland to complete World Cup semifinal lineup
stories.summary: Defending champions Argentina beat Switzerland 3-1 after extra time in Kansas City on Saturday, July 11, with Alexis Mac Allister, Julian Alvarez and Lautaro Martinez scoring, to complete the 2026 World Cup semifinal lineup. Argentina will face England, while Spain will play France in the other semifinal. Spain, France and England had advanced with quarterfinal wins over Belgium, Morocco and Norway respectively.
stories.watch_for: In the semifinals, Spain play France on Tuesday and Argentina face England in Atlanta on Wednesday.
